course_title: Indigenous Politics within and beyond the state

learning_outcomes.description: Identify Indigenous and predominant western approaches to politics at play in political relations, in policy frameworks and strategies, and in contested or conflictual political debates.
Understand key historical dynamics and relations between Indigenous peoples and states/mainstream populations.
Identify possible pathways and practices for improving political relations and policy-making between Indigenous and non-Indigenous peoples.
Critically reflect upon how existing knowledge is disrupted, confirmed or extended amid the contestations that accompany knowing about Indigenous politics.

assessments.task_description:
Task

You are to complete a 150 word (approximately) relflection on the seminar discussion each week (excluding weeks 6 and 13). You will be expected to discuss how your understanding of the topic is evolving, how your interpretaton of the reading/s have changed during the discussion, and how your learning is progressing. To complete this task you should engage with a) at least one of the required readings and the pre-recorded video, b) the class discussion (comments and interpretations of your colleagues and course staff) and c) the course Learning Outcomes while reflecting upon how you are coming to know. Rather than aiming primarily to demonstrate what you know, you should also engage in self-analysis and critique.

Your reflection is to be answered individually in handwritten form in class. Paper will be provided. No electronic devices are permitted to be used. Your final mark for this item will be based upon your best 8 contributions.

Course staff will guide and support you to participate in this novel form of assessment. They will advise you on how to prepare you for this task, and provide you with additionional in-class prompts to help focus your reflection.

If a medical or other circumstance leads to challenges for you completing this assessment item, please contact the course coordinator directly.

Please consult the criteria sheet for this item.

Background

Participation in the seminars is an essential part of this course and is directly linked with the course Learning Outcomes (see Aims and Outcomes section). Our best learning often happens through reflection. This can occur when we consider the challenges of knowing amidst contested political relations, are open with ourselves and others about the processes that accompany our learning, and reflect upon how our existing knowledge is disrupted, confirmed or extended. Consider, for instance, that our relationship with knowing can be emotional. Learning may be challenging, confronting, affirming, surprising, or frustrating. You should aim to openly discuss these and other experiences in engaging with the topic (including readings and pre-recorded videos) with your class colleagues, and link these with your experience of learning.

Please Note:

This assessment task is to be completed in person. The use of generative Artificial Intelligence (AI) or Machine Translation (MT) tools will not be permitted. Any attempt to use AI or MT may constitute student misconduct under the Student Code of Conduct.
assessments.submission_guidelines: Your reflection is to be answered individually in handwritten form, and handed in at the end of the class. Paper will be provided. No electronic devices are permitted to be used.
assessments.deferral_or_extension: You cannot defer or apply for an extension for this assessment.
assessments.late_submission: There is no provision for late submission for this item as it must be completed in class.
assessments.additional_info: You cannot defer or apply for an extension for this assessment.

assessments.assessment_title: Planning for Issue Analysis and Commentary Paper 20%
assessments.task_description:
Task

You are required prepare a 1200 word written document to a) identify an audience for one of the topics outlined in the 'Issue Analysis and Commentary paper: Topics and guidance' document, b) design an aligned communication strategy for your paper, and c) make a structured plan for your paper. You should explain who you are targeting and why they are a relevant target for your chosen topic, specify your strategy for communicating the complexity and challenge implicit in your topic to the target audience, and map how you will structure content to inform and persuade your audience. You should aim to structure your document using headings to reflect the three core components.

Please consult the criteria sheet for this item.

Background

Effective communication regarding complex matters requires careful strategic planning. Understanding your audience and devising an effective communication strategy is especially important.

This task is designed to help you prepare for the final assessment task, the Issue Analysis and Commentary Paper. To begin, please read the task description for that item and the related document 'Issue Analysis and Commentary paper: Topics and guidance'.

This assessment item allows course staff to provide you with feedback on the Issue Analysis and Commentary Paper at the planning stage. Our goal is to support you to develop your submission for the final assessment task.

Please note:

Artificial Intelligence (AI) and Machine Translation (MT) are emerging tools that may support students in completing this assessment task. Students may appropriately use AI or MT in completing this assessment task. You must clearly reference any use of AI or MT in each instance through the use of the AI Planner as part of your submission - include it after the title page.

A failure to reference generative AI or MT use may constitute student misconduct under the Student Code of Conduct.
assessments.submission_guidelines: You may be able to apply for an extension.
assessments.deferral_or_extension: You may be able to apply for an extension.
assessments.late_submission: A penalty of 10% of the maximum possible mark will be deducted per 24 hours from time submission is due for up to 7 days. After 7 days, you will receive a mark of 0.
assessments.additional_info: You may be able to apply for an extension.

assessments.task_description:
Task

You are to conceptualise, prepare and submit an Issue Analysis and Commentary paper. Possible topics and guidance are listed in the relevant Blackboard folder (there is an option to develop your own topic). Depending on the work you have done in the previous assessment item, you may be writing for a government minister, informing a leader in a non-government organistion, or informing the work of an advocacy organisation. Your submission should aim be balanced and informed by argumentation and evidence (including existing scholarship) yet not at the expense of avoiding challenging or contentious matters. This is a text-based assessment; your submission should contain no images.

Remember to build upon your work in the Planning Document and to carefully target your audience.

Please consult the Marking Criteria for this item.

Background

Researchers and advocates use written texts to inform and persuade decision-makers and policy-makers, sometimes by advocating for their or their constituents' or point of view. The targeted policy- and decision-makers are often - but not always - politicians and public servants. These texts are variously called discussion papers, options papers, policy briefs, issue briefs and other terms too. There are no exact rules about how these types of texts are constructed, but they usually have key goals of raising a challenging or complex issue, condensing the issue into a brief and accessible format, and advocating for a reasoned and/or evidence-based response in relation to the issue.

This assessment item builds on the Planning Document.

Please note:

Artificial Intelligence (AI) and Machine Translation (MT) are emerging tools that may support students in completing this assessment task. Students may appropriately use AI or MT in completing this assessment task. You must clearly reference any use of AI or MT in each instance through the use of the AI Planner as partof your submission - include it after the title page.

A failure to reference generative AI or MT use may constitute student misconduct under the Student Code of Conduct.
assessments.deferral_or_extension: You may be able to apply for an extension.
assessments.late_submission: A penalty of 10% of the maximum possible mark will be deducted per 24 hours from time submission is due for up to 7 days. After 7 days, you will receive a mark of 0.
assessments.additional_info: You may be able to apply for an extension.

learning_activities.topic:
Week 1 - Position, Place, Relation

Introduction to the course and each other. Individual positioning in relation to land, ways of knowing, political relations, study area, and particular topics/interests.
